title: South Pacific Tuna Treaty Act of 2025
short_title: South Pacific Tuna Treaty Act of 2025
stage_label: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.
plain_summary: This law updates rules for American fishing boats that catch tuna in Pacific Ocean waters near island countries. The changes come from a treaty agreement made in 2016 between the United States and 16 Pacific Island nations. The law changes how fishing boats get licenses, what they are not allowed to do, and how the rules are enforced. It also changes what information the fishing boats must report and removes some old rules that are no longer needed.
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the South Pacific Tuna Treaty Act of twenty twenty five. The law has seventeen sections that cover topics like definitions, prohibited acts, exceptions, criminal offenses, civil penalties, licenses, enforcement, findings by Secretary, reporting requirements, closed area stowage requirements, observers, technical assistance, arbitration, disposition of fees and penalties, and additional agreements.

sections.title: Section 2 Amendment of South Pacific Tuna Act of 1988
sections.text: When this law mentions changing or removing a section, it means changing or removing a section from the South Pacific Tuna Act of nineteen eighty eight. This older law is found in section sixteen of the United States Code starting at section nine hundred seventy three.

sections.title: Section 3 Definitions
sections.text: This section changes the meanings of important terms used in the law. Applicable National Law now means laws that are properly announced and in effect. Closed Area now means any area under control of a Pacific Island country that is closed to fishing vessels because of that country's laws that are properly announced and in effect. The definition of Fishing now includes harvesting fish for any purpose and using any vessel, vehicle, aircraft, or hovercraft for fishing activities except during emergencies involving crew health or safety or vessel safety. Fishing Vessel now specifically means vessels used for commercial purse seine fishing for tuna instead of just commercial fishing. Licensing Area now means areas under control of Pacific Island countries except for internal waters, territorial seas, archipelagic waters, and any Closed Areas. The law removes the definitions of Limited Area, Party, and Treaty Area and renumbers the remaining definitions. It adds a new definition for Regional Terms and Conditions, which means any terms or conditions that the Administrator attaches to fishing licenses and the Secretary announces.

sections.title: Section 4 Prohibited Acts
sections.text: This section changes the list of things that fishing vessels cannot do. It removes some old prohibited acts and renumbers the remaining ones. It adds that vessels cannot enter Closed Areas unless they have an agreement under the Treaty. The law adds two new prohibited acts: violating any regional terms and conditions, and violating any limits on authorized fishing effort or catch. For acts in the Licensing Area, the law removes one prohibited act and renumbers the remaining ones.

sections.title: Section 5 Exceptions
sections.text: This section completely removes the exceptions section from the original law. There are no longer any general exceptions to the prohibited acts.

sections.title: Section 6 Criminal Offenses
sections.text: This section updates which prohibited acts can result in criminal charges. The law changes the section references to match the new numbering system created in earlier sections.

sections.title: Section 7 Civil Penalties
sections.text: This section changes the rules about civil penalties (fines). It removes some specific language about certain types of violations and updates section references to match the new numbering. It also removes language about Limited Areas being closed to fishing.

sections.title: Section 8 Licenses
sections.text: This section makes major changes to how fishing licenses work. The Secretary must forward license applications to the Administrator when they follow proper procedures, except in certain cases. Fees must be paid according to the Treaty and procedures set by the Secretary. The Secretary can refuse to forward an application if it doesn't follow the Treaty or procedures, or if the owner or charterer is in bankruptcy without financial assurances, lacks proper maritime insurance, or hasn't paid final penalties. The law updates various legal references and clarifies that licensed vessels can fish in specific areas but cannot intentionally use nets to encircle dolphins or other marine mammals while fishing.

sections.title: Section 9 Enforcement
sections.text: This section updates enforcement procedures by removing specific references to certain Treaty articles while keeping the basic enforcement framework. It makes small technical changes to the language about searches and seizures by authorized officers.

sections.title: Section 10 Findings by Secretary
sections.text: This section updates the procedures for when the Secretary orders vessels to leave certain waters. It removes references to Limited Areas and updates section references to match the new numbering system. The law also removes specific Treaty article references while keeping the basic framework for ordering vessels to leave waters when Pacific Island countries are investigating alleged Treaty violations.

sections.title: Section 11 Reporting Requirements and Disclosure of Information
sections.text: This section completely rewrites the reporting rules. The Secretary must keep certain information confidential, including information from the Administrator marked as confidential, information collected by observers, and information submitted by anyone following this law. This information is exempt from Freedom of Information Act requests. However, the Secretary can share this information in specific cases: when ordered by a court, for enforcement purposes, for Coast Guard homeland and national security missions, for Treaty administration or fishery management, with the Administrator as required by the Treaty, with international fisheries organizations following their rules and agreements to protect identities, when the person providing the information gives written permission, or in summary form that doesn't reveal individual identities. The law includes a savings clause stating that nothing limits Congress's authority to obtain any record or information.

sections.title: Section 12 Closed Area Stowage Requirements
sections.text: This section simplifies the rules about how fishing equipment must be stored when vessels are in Closed Areas. Instead of specific detailed requirements, vessels must follow any requirements established by the Secretary.

sections.title: Section 13 Observers
sections.text: This section completely removes the observers section from the original law.

sections.title: Section 14 Technical Assistance
sections.text: This section rewrites the technical assistance provisions. The Secretary and Secretary of State can provide assistance to Pacific Island countries to help them benefit from fisheries development and licensed fishing vessel operations. This assistance can include technical help, training and capacity building opportunities, helping implement private sector activities or partnerships, and other activities the Secretaries determine are appropriate.

sections.title: Section 15 Arbitration
sections.text: This section updates the arbitration procedures by removing specific Treaty article references. The arbitral tribunal under the Treaty will determine where arbitration takes place.

sections.title: Section 16 Disposition of Fees, Penalties, Forfeitures, and Other Moneys
sections.text: This section makes a small change by removing a specific Treaty article reference while keeping the basic framework for handling fees, penalties, forfeitures, and other money.

sections.title: Section 17 Additional Agreements
sections.text: This section changes how additional fishing agreements work. Instead of specific notification requirements, the Secretary can establish procedures for reviewing any agreements for additional fishing access made under the Treaty.
